Relowa POC · v0.1
Home / Concepts / Hash Anchoring

Hash Anchoring on Arbitrum One

TypeConcept
💡

Why we publish a 32-byte Merkle root to an L2 every day, and how an auditor verifies it independently.

What this is

📋

Hash anchoring is a compliance primitive, not a blockchain product. A daily Lambda computes a Merkle root from 24 hours of audit data → calls an Arbitrum One smart contract → stores the root on-chain. This proves, cryptographically and timestamped, that "at block height H, this exact dataset existed" — without revealing any of the data itself.

An auditor gets the raw data from us. They recompute the Merkle root independently. If it matches the on-chain root, they know that the data they hold is the same data that existed at that point in time — not a later fabrication.

Three layers of audit protection

LayerProtects againstTrust model
DB hash chain Retroactive row insertion/deletion/modification Trust the DB operator. SHA-256 makes tampering detectable within seconds.
S3 WORM mirror DB operator collusion (operator + DBA alter rows and recompute hash chain) Trust AWS Object Lock enforcement. Data cannot be deleted or modified after write.
Arbitrum anchoring AWS + Relowa collusion (S3 object modified, WORM lock bypassed via root account) Trust the Ethereum L1 consensus. A single honest Arbitrum validator makes forgery economically impossible.

How it works — Anchoring

Every 24 hours, an EventBridge Scheduler triggers a Lambda. Here's what it does:

  1. Queries audit_events and certificates for the past 24 hours (UTC midnight to midnight).
  2. For each row, computes a Merkle leaf: sha256(id|org_id|user_id|action|...|prev_hash).
  3. Builds a Merkle tree from all leaves of that day.
  4. Extracts the 32-byte Merkle root.
  5. Loads the Arbitrum contract ABI and a funded wallet from Secrets Manager.
  6. Calls contract.anchorRoot(date, merkleRoot) on Arbitrum One.
  7. Logs the transaction hash, block number, and Merkle root to the anchor_log table.

How it works — Verification

An auditor (or our own compliance team) can independently verify any day's data:

  1. Request the raw audit data for date D from Relowa.
  2. Recompute the Merkle tree from the data using the same leaf construction algorithm (published in the ADR).
  3. Compare the resulting root against the on-chain value at contract.getRoot(date).
  4. If they match: the data is authentic and existed at the time of the on-chain transaction.
  5. If they don't match: the data was altered after anchoring, or the wrong dataset was provided.

The auditor needs zero blockchain knowledge — they can use a block explorer to read the stored root, or we can provide it with a Merkle proof.

What goes into Merkle leaves

Each leaf is a deterministic SHA-256 hash of a single row's fields, pipe-delimited:

-- audit_events leaf
sha256(
  id || '|' || org_id || '|' || user_id || '|' ||
  action || '|' || resource_type || '|' ||
  resource_id || '|' || new_values || '|' ||
  created_at || '|' || prev_hash
)

-- certificates leaf
sha256(
  id || '|' || org_id || '|' || tonnage || '|' ||
  material_type || '|' || carbon_factor || '|' ||
  issued_at || '|' || prev_hash
)

The two trees are computed separately, then their roots are hashed together to produce a single daily root. This means an auditor can verify just audit data, just certificate data, or both.

Why Arbitrum One

FactorWhy it matters
L2 on EthereumInherits Ethereum's security. Data availability and fraud proofs are guaranteed by L1 validators. No need to trust a sidechain's validator set.
EVM-compatibleThe anchoring contract is a simple Solidity mapping: mapping(uint256 => bytes32) public roots;. No new language, no new tooling, no new attack surface.
~$0.01 per dayEven at 100× gas spikes, anchoring 32 bytes once per day costs under a dollar. This is a compliance checkbox, not a profit center — the cost must be negligible.
No single-company sequencerArbitrum has a decentralized validator set with a permissionless challenge period. A single malicious sequencer cannot forge a root without being challenged and slashed.

What we intentionally DON'T do

🛑
  • No ZK proofs. Merkle trees achieve the same "prove inclusion without revealing data" property with far less complexity. ZK would add a compiler toolchain, a proving circuit, and months of audit overhead for zero additional compliance benefit.
  • No per-event anchoring. We batch 24 hours. Per-event would require a transaction for every tender, bid, and certificate — thousands of dollars per day in gas.
  • No tokens. There is no RELOWA token, no staking, no governance token. This is infrastructure, not a tokenomics project.
  • No data on-chain. The contract stores a 32-byte hash, not the data. The data lives in our Postgres DB and S3. The chain is a trust anchor, not a data store.
  • No client wallets. End users never interact with Arbitrum. They never see a transaction, sign a message, or hold ETH. The anchoring is purely server-side.

ESG verification flow

✅
End-to-end CSRD auditor scenario

A third-party auditor is verifying a producer's 2025 carbon offset claims for CSRD compliance. Here's the full flow:

  1. Auditor requests "all waste certificates for Org X in 2025."
  2. Relowa exports the certificate rows + the anchor_log entries for each day those certificates were anchored.
  3. Auditor recomputes each day's Merkle tree from the raw certificate data.
  4. Auditor checks the computed root against the on-chain root (via Arbiscan or an RPC call).
  5. Auditor verifies that the on-chain transaction timestamp is within the reporting period.
  6. Auditor traces the hash chain backward from the last certificate to the first, confirming no gaps.
  7. Auditor signs off: "Certificate data for Org X in 2025 is cryptographically verified as authentic and tamper-free."

Operational notes

ComponentDetails
Trigger EventBridge Scheduler — cron at 00:05 UTC daily. 5-minute buffer ensures all of yesterday's events are committed.
Failure alert CloudWatch alarm on Lambda error rate > 0, or missing anchor_log row for today's date. If anchoring fails, we have a ~24-hour window to retry before the day's data is no longer "yesterday."
anchor_log table Records: (date, merkle_root, tx_hash, block_number, anchored_at). This table itself is part of the audit hash chain — the anchoring action is itself an auditable event.
Wallet management The Arbitrum wallet private key lives in AWS Secrets Manager. The Lambda's execution role has secretsmanager:GetSecretValue scoped to that specific secret. No .env files, no hardcoded keys.
Gas strategy No priority fees. The transaction can be included in any block within the 24-hour window. If gas exceeds a threshold, the Lambda retries with exponential backoff.
See also
